lessonsLearned.lesson: Every piece of context must have exactly one owner. When ownership is ambiguous, context drifts silently.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Apply when creating or reviewing any context data point

lessonsLearned.lesson: Context rot is the natural state. Without active maintenance, all business context degrades over time.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Use this as motivation for regular maintenance cycles

lessonsLearned.lesson: The fastest way to detect context rot is to find the same information in two places and check if they still agree.
Start with 3-5 data points. Add more only after the first set is actively maintained and useful.
Infrastructure must be wired into workflow steps to be useful. A maintenance checklist that nobody follows is worse than no checklist.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When designing or reviewing maintenance processes

lessonsLearned.lesson: When the same concept has different names in different data points, comparison and analysis become unreliable.
lessonsLearned.applicability: During context reviews and cross-data-point audits

lessonsLearned.lesson: Consolidate AFTER pattern is proven stable. Premature abstraction creates complexity without value.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When deciding whether to merge or restructure data points

lessonsLearned.lesson: Reference the source, don't copy it. When you need information from another data point, link to it instead of duplicating the content.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When writing data point content that touches another data point's domain

lessonsLearned.lesson: Discovery scripts are the source of truth for what exists. State files record what discovery found, not the definition itself.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When checking ecosystem inventory or resolving discrepancies

lessonsLearned.lesson: Even with mandatory integration audits in FIXED END, ecosystem drift accumulates from ad-hoc changes between planned sessions. A full cross-reference audit found 12 issues (3 critical) that individual file-level checks missed — stale registries, missing entries, wrong paths.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Run a full ecosystem audit (not just per-file integration check) after every batch of changes, especially when multiple files were touched across sessions

lessonsLearned.lesson: File hash comparisons must normalize line endings (CRLF vs LF). Windows stores CRLF, GitHub zips contain LF — identical content produces different SHA256 hashes without normalization. This caused 70 false 'modified' results in the update script.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Any script that compares file content across platforms (update.py, integration checks)

lessonsLearned.lesson: Start maintenance schedules at aggressive frequency (daily/weekly). If you default to monthly or quarterly, users forget the system exists before it ever fires. Let them dial back when checks consistently find nothing — that's the signal things are stable.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When setting up scheduled maintenance for any new BCOS installation

lessonsLearned.lesson: Never limit onboarding to a fixed number of data points. Analyze ALL content, classify by document type (context/process/policy/reference/playbook), and create as many data points as needed. Arbitrary limits (like '3 data points') lose information and misclassify mixed content.
lessonsLearned.applicability: During context-onboarding, context-ingest, or any bulk content processing

lessonsLearned.lesson: After squash-merging a PR from dev to main on GitHub, dev branch still shows as 'ahead' because commit SHAs differ despite identical content. Must force-push main back to dev after every PR merge: `git push public public/main:dev --force`.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Every time a PR is merged on the public repo — use `publish.sh --sync`

lessonsLearned.lesson: File names are free in Glob results — Claude sees paths without spending tokens on content. Consistent naming conventions (YYYY-MM-DD_entity_topic.ext) let Claude filter by date or entity via Glob patterns alone. The Document Index with DOMAIN one-liners is the second routing layer: Claude scans 20 one-liners instead of opening 20 files. Reserve full file reads for the 2-3 docs that actually matter.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When designing folder structures, naming conventions, or navigation aids for any content-heavy repo
